content:
Ideas about **fitting flexibility** vs **stability** appear early in **statistics** — choosing polynomial degree or smoothing bandwidth balances **bias** and **variance** of estimators.

**Stein's paradox** and **shrinkage estimators** (1950s–1970s) showed deliberately **reducing effective complexity** can improve prediction — foreshadowing regularization.

**Hoerl and Kennard's ridge regression** (1970) formalized trading higher **bias** for lower **variance** by constraining coefficient magnitude — complexity control without changing feature count.

**Vapnik–Chervonenkis theory** and **structural risk minimization** (1970s–1990s) linked **hypothesis class capacity** to generalization bounds, motivating **complexity penalties** in SVMs and later deep learning theory.

**Hastie, Tibshirani, and Friedman's** *Elements of Statistical Learning* (2001) popularized **validation curves** and **model selection** over complexity hyperparameters for practitioners.

Modern **AutoML**, **neural architecture search**, and **scaling laws** extend the same question: **how much capacity does this dataset justify?** — with **model complexity** as the operational knob on the **bias–variance tradeoff**.

timeline.label: Classical smoothing and degree choice (early 20th century)
timeline.description: Statisticians select bandwidth or polynomial order — early bias–variance intuition for flexible fits.

timeline.label: Ridge regression (1970)
timeline.description: Hoerl & Kennard shrink effective complexity via L2 penalty — explicit bias–variance trade.

timeline.label: VC theory & SRM (1970s–1990s)
timeline.description: Capacity measures link hypothesis class size to generalization; motivates complexity control.

timeline.label: ESL model selection (2001)
timeline.description: Validation curves over tree size, spline df, and penalty strength become standard ML practice.

timeline.label: AutoML & scaling (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Automated sweeps over architecture and depth operationalize complexity tuning at scale.
